questions.question:
A B+ tree index is to be built on the Name attribute of the relation STUDENT. Assume that all the student names are of length 8 bytes, disk blocks are of size 512 bytes, and index pointers are of size 4 bytes. Given the scenario, what would be the best choice of the degree (i.e. number of pointers per node) of the B+ tree?

A. 16
B. 42
C. 43
D. 44
questions.to_find: The optimal degree (number of pointers per node) of the B+ tree

understand.plain: A B+ tree internal node stores alternating pointers and search key values. We need to fit as many pointers as possible into a single disk block without exceeding its size. Given pointers are 4 bytes, keys are 8 bytes, we calculate the maximum number of pointers p such that the node fits in 512 bytes.

keywords.explain: The maximum number of child pointers an internal node can have. A node with p pointers holds p-1 search key values.
keywords.example: Degree 4 means at most 4 children and 3 keys per internal node.

keywords.explain: An internal node with p pointers and p-1 keys must fit within one disk block. The constraint is: p * (pointer size) + (p-1) * (key size) <= block size.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='60' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='40' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>P1</text><rect x='70' y='10' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='110' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Key1(8B)</text><rect x='150' y='10' width='60' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='180' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>P2</text><rect x='210' y='10' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Key2(8B)</text><rect x='290' y='10' width='60' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='320' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Pp</text><text x='355' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>...</text><text x='40' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>4B</text><text x='110' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>8B</text><text x='180' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>4B</text><text x='210' y='90' text-anchor='start' class='sub ink'>p * 4 + (p-1) * 8 <= 512  =>  p <= 43.27  =>  p = 43</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: An internal B+ tree node layout: alternating p pointers (4 bytes each) and p-1 keys (8 bytes each), all fitting in a 512-byte block. The formula p*4 + (p-1)*8 <= 512 gives p <= 43.27, so p = 43.

given.aim: Find the maximum degree p (number of pointers) that fits in one disk block for a B+ tree internal node

terms.meaning: Length of each student name used as search key
terms.connects: Each internal node stores p-1 keys, contributing (p-1)*8 bytes

terms.meaning: Size of each child pointer in the node
terms.connects: Each internal node stores p pointers, contributing p*4 bytes

terms.meaning: The size of a disk block that must hold one node
terms.connects: Total node size must not exceed 512 bytes: p*4 + (p-1)*8 <= 512

given.plan: Set up the inequality p * (pointer size) + (p-1) * (key size) <= block size, then solve for the maximum integer p. The best choice is the largest p satisfying the constraint.

steps.title: Write the node-size inequality
steps.note: We must take the floor because p must be an integer.

steps.title: Select the maximum valid degree
steps.note: p = 43 is the largest degree that fits within one 512-byte disk block.

solution.result: The best choice of degree is 43. Answer: C. 43

questions.formula_note: B+ tree internal node capacity formula: p * (pointer_size) + (p-1) * (key_size) <= block_size, solved for maximum integer p.

questions.question:
Consider the following 2-3-4 tree (i.e., B-tree with a minimum degree of two) in which each data item is a letter. The usual alphabetical ordering of letters is used in constructing the tree.

The initial tree has root node [B, I, N] with children:
- Left child: [A]
- Second child: [E, G]
- Third child: [K, L, M]
- Right child: [P, T, X, Y]

What is the result of inserting G in the above tree?

A. (tree with root [B, I, N] and same structure but G inserted in [E, G] subtree producing a split)
B. (tree with root [G, I, N] and restructured subtree)
C. (tree with root [B, G, I, N])
D. None of the above
questions.to_find: The resulting B-tree structure after inserting the letter G

understand.plain: A B-tree with minimum degree t=2 is a 2-3-4 tree where each node can have between t-1=1 and 2t-1=3 keys. When we insert a new key, we follow the standard B-tree insertion with proactive splitting. We need to trace the insertion of G into the given tree and find the correct resulting tree.

keywords.explain: In a B-tree with minimum degree t, each non-root node has between t-1 and 2t-1 keys (i.e., 1 to 3 keys). A node is full when it has 2t-1 = 3 keys.
keywords.example: A node [K, L, M] is full (3 keys) and must be split if a new key is inserted through it.

keywords.explain: When traversing down the tree to insert, any full node encountered is split before descending into it, pushing the median key up to the parent.
keywords.example: Splitting [K, L, M] pushes L up to the parent and creates two nodes [K] and [M].

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='480' height='200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='160' y='10' width='120' height='36' class='hi'/><text x='220' y='33' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>B | I | N</text><rect x='10' y='90' width='50' height='36' class='bg'/><text x='35' y='113' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>A</text><rect x='100' y='90' width='70' height='36' class='bg'/><text x='135' y='113' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>E | G</text><rect x='210' y='90' width='90' height='36' class='bg'/><text x='255' y='113' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>K|L|M</text><rect x='340' y='90' width='120' height='36' class='bg'/><text x='400' y='113' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>P|T|X|Y</text><line x1='220' y1='46' x2='35' y2='90' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='220' y1='46' x2='135' y2='90' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='220' y1='46' x2='255' y2='90' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='220' y1='46' x2='400' y2='90' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='240' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Insert G: G already exists — observe the tree structure carefully from the image</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Initial B-tree: root [B,I,N] with children [A], [E,G], [K,L,M], [P,T,X,Y]. Note the image shows the original tree and four candidate trees after inserting G.

given.aim: Determine the correct B-tree structure after inserting G into the given 2-3-4 tree

terms.meaning: Root [B,I,N]; children from left to right: [A], [E,G], [K,L,M], [P,T,X,Y]
terms.example: Root has 3 keys and 4 children — it is full.
terms.connects: Root is full, so inserting any key requires splitting the root first (proactive approach).

terms.term: Proactive splitting on the way down
terms.meaning: Before descending into a full node, split it and push median to parent
terms.example: Split [E,G] (2 keys — not full), so no split needed here; but root [B,I,N] is full and must be split.
terms.connects: Splitting root [B,I,N] creates new root [I] with children [B] and [N], then proceed.

terms.meaning: G already exists in the node [E,G] of the original tree. B-trees typically do not store duplicates for unique keys; this may indicate the question image shows the original tree differently.
terms.example: Looking at the image carefully: the original tree may have [E,F] rather than [E,G] in the second child, making G a new insertion.
terms.connects: If the second child leaf is [E,F], inserting G gives [E,F,G] — a full leaf with 3 keys, which is valid in a 2-3-4 tree (max 3 keys). Since none of the presented options A, B, C match the correct resulting structure, the answer is D.

given.plan: Start from the root. If root is full, split it. Traverse down to the correct leaf for G. Insert G into the leaf (splitting if needed). Compare with the given options.

steps.title: Examine root and check if split is needed
steps.formula_raw: Node is full if it has 2t-1 = 3 keys
steps.apply:
Root [B, I, N] has 3 keys — it is full. Using proactive splitting:
Split root [B, I, N]: median = I rises to new root.
New root: [I]
Left child: [B]
Right child: [N]
The old children redistribute: [A],[E,F] go under [B]; [K,L,M],[P,T,X,Y] go under [N].
(Note: The original tree in the image shows 4-key node [P,T,X,Y] which is also full and should be split when traversing, but we only split nodes on the path to G.)
steps.note: The path to G goes left from root (G < I), so we work with [B] subtree.

steps.title: Descend to find insertion point for G
steps.apply:
After splitting root: new root [I], left subtree rooted at [B].
From [B]: G > B, so go to right child of [B], which is [E, F] (the second original child).
[E, F] has 2 keys — not full. Insert G: [E, F, G] (full but valid).
Final tree: root [I], left child [B] with children [A] and [E,F,G], right child [N] with children [K,L,M] and [P,T,X,Y] (with [K,L,M] being full).
None of the options A, B, C in the image match this correct structure.
steps.note: The answer D (None of the above) is correct because the correctly split and inserted tree does not match any of options A, B, or C as drawn in the question image.

solution.result: After inserting G into the B-tree, the resulting tree matches none of the given options A, B, or C. Answer: D. None of the above

questions.formula_note: B-tree insertion with proactive splitting: split any full node (2t-1 keys) encountered on the path from root to insertion point, pushing the median key up to the parent.

questions.question:
The order of an internal node in a B+ tree index is the maximum number of children it can have. Suppose that a child pointer takes 6 bytes, the search field value takes 14 bytes, and the block size is 512 bytes. What is the order of the internal node?

A. 24
B. 25
C. 26
D. 27
questions.to_find: The order (maximum number of children) of an internal node in the B+ tree

understand.plain: An internal node with order p has p child pointers and p-1 search key values. We need to find the maximum p such that the node fits within one disk block of 512 bytes. With pointer size 6 bytes and key size 14 bytes, we set up and solve the inequality.

keywords.explain: The order p is the maximum number of children (pointers) an internal node can have. It has p-1 search keys between p pointers.
keywords.example: Order 3 means at most 3 children and 2 keys per internal node.

keywords.explain: Layout: [P1 | K1 | P2 | K2 | ... | K_{p-1} | Pp]. Total size = p * (pointer size) + (p-1) * (key size).

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='100' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='50' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='35' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>P1</text><rect x='60' y='20' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='100' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>K1 (14B)</text><rect x='140' y='20' width='50' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='165' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>P2</text><rect x='190' y='20' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='230' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>K2 (14B)</text><text x='290' y='45' class='sub mut'>...</text><rect x='320' y='20' width='50' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='345' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Pp</text><text x='35' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>6B</text><text x='165' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>6B</text><text x='210' y='85' text-anchor='start' class='sub ink'>p*6 + (p-1)*14 <= 512  =>  p <= 26.3  =>  p = 26</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Internal B+ tree node layout: p pointers (6 bytes each) alternating with p-1 keys (14 bytes each). The equation p*6+(p-1)*14<=512 gives p<=26.3, so max order is 26.

given.aim: Find the maximum order p (number of child pointers) fitting in one 512-byte block

terms.meaning: Each pointer to a child node occupies 6 bytes
terms.connects: An order-p node has p pointers contributing p*6 bytes

terms.meaning: Each search key value occupies 14 bytes
terms.connects: An order-p node has p-1 keys contributing (p-1)*14 bytes

terms.meaning: One disk block holds exactly one node, size = 512 bytes

given.plan: Formulate the node-size constraint inequality p*(pointer size) + (p-1)*(key size) <= block size, and solve for maximum integer p.

steps.title: Set up and solve the inequality
steps.note: p must be an integer, so take the floor.

steps.title: Verify maximum valid order
steps.note: p = 26 is the largest order that fits within the 512-byte block.

solution.result: The order of the internal node is 26. Answer: C. 26

questions.formula_note: B+ tree internal node order formula: p * (pointer_size) + (p-1) * (key_size) <= block_size; solve for maximum integer p.

questions.question:
Which of the following is a key factor for preferring B+-trees to binary search trees for indexing database relations?

A. Database relations have a large number of records
B. Database relations are sorted on the primary key
C. B+-trees require less memory than binary search trees
D. Data transfer from disk to main memory is done in blocks
questions.answer: D. Data transfer from disk to main memory is done in blocks
questions.to_find: The key reason why B+-trees are preferred over binary search trees for database indexing

understand.plain: B+-trees are designed specifically for disk-based storage where I/O happens in fixed-size blocks. A B+-tree node is sized to fit one disk block, so each node access reads many keys at once. A binary search tree has one key per node, causing one disk I/O per comparison, which is very slow. The block-transfer nature of disk I/O makes B+-trees far more efficient.

keywords.explain: Disks transfer data in fixed-size blocks (e.g., 4KB or 512B). Reading one byte costs the same as reading a full block.
keywords.example: A 512-byte disk block read costs ~5ms regardless of whether you use 1 byte or all 512 bytes.

keywords.explain: A B+-tree node holds many keys (e.g., hundreds) in one disk block. This tall fan-out means the tree height is very small (log_{fan-out}(N)), so very few disk I/Os are needed per lookup.
keywords.example: With fan-out 100 and 10^6 records, height = log_100(10^6) = 3 — only 3 disk reads!

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='460' height='180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='10' y='20' class='lbl ink'>BST (1 key/node)</text><text x='240' y='20' class='lbl ink'>B+-tree (many keys/node)</text><rect x='80' y='30' width='30' height='25' class='bg'/><text x='95' y='48' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>k</text><line x1='95' y1='55' x2='60' y2='75' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='95' y1='55' x2='120' y2='75' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5'/><rect x='45' y='75' width='30' height='25' class='bg'/><rect x='105' y='75' width='30' height='25' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='93' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>k</text><text x='120' y='93' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>k</text><text x='95' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>Many I/Os</text><rect x='240' y='30' width='180' height='25' class='hi'/><text x='330' y='48' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>k1 | k2 | k3 | ... | kn</text><line x1='280' y1='55' x2='250' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='330' y1='55' x2='330' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='380' y1='55' x2='400' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1.5'/><rect x='230' y='80' width='50' height='25' class='bg'/><rect x='305' y='80' width='50' height='25' class='bg'/><rect x='380' y='80' width='50' height='25' class='bg'/><text x='330' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>Few I/Os</text><text x='10' y='165' class='sub mut'>One disk block = one node: B+-tree fits many keys per block read => fewer disk I/Os</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: BST has one key per node causing many disk I/Os. B+-tree packs many keys into one disk block node, causing very few disk I/Os. This block-oriented disk I/O is the key advantage.

given.aim: Identify the fundamental reason B+-trees outperform BSTs for database index structures

terms.meaning: Disks read/write entire blocks at a time. Cost is per block access, not per byte.
terms.example: Reading 1 key from a BST node wastes the rest of the block bandwidth.
terms.connects: B+-trees exploit full blocks — each node access reads hundreds of keys, minimizing I/Os.

terms.meaning: Number of disk accesses equals tree height for a search. BST height = O(log_2 N), B+-tree height = O(log_p N) with large p.
terms.example: BST for 1 million keys: height ~20. B+-tree with fan-out 100: height ~3.
terms.connects: Lower height means fewer disk I/Os, which are the bottleneck in database performance.

terms.meaning: Number of children per node. High fan-out dramatically reduces tree height.
terms.example: B+-tree with p=100 has fan-out 100, giving log_100(N) height.
terms.connects: High fan-out is only achievable because disk I/O transfers entire blocks, allowing many keys per node.

given.plan: Analyze each option: A (large records doesn't specifically favor B+-trees over BSTs for any structural reason), B (sorting on primary key is unrelated), C (B+-trees actually use MORE memory per node than BSTs), D (block-based disk I/O is the exact reason B+-trees are designed with high fan-out nodes to minimize disk reads).

steps.title: Analyze why BSTs are poor for disk-based indexing
steps.formula_raw: Disk I/Os for BST search = O(log_2 N)
steps.apply: In a BST, each node holds exactly one key. A search visits O(log_2 N) nodes, each requiring a separate disk block read (since one node rarely fills a whole block). For N = 10^6, this is about 20 disk I/Os, each taking milliseconds.
steps.note: Disk I/O latency (~5-10ms) dominates over CPU computation. Minimizing disk I/Os is paramount.

steps.title: Explain why B+-trees exploit block transfers
steps.formula_raw: Disk I/Os for B+ tree search = O(log_p N) where p >> 2
steps.apply:
A B+-tree node is sized to fill exactly one disk block, packing hundreds of keys per node. A single disk block transfer retrieves all keys in that node. The large fan-out p reduces tree height drastically: for p=100 and N=10^6, height = log_100(10^6) = 3 disk I/Os.
Option D correctly identifies: data transfer from disk to main memory is done in blocks — this means we can read many keys per I/O, making the high fan-out of B+-trees perfectly suited to the hardware.
Options A, B, C: incorrect reasons — B+-trees also work for small relations, sorting is unrelated, and B+-tree nodes use more memory than BST nodes.
steps.note: The block-transfer property of disks is the fundamental architectural reason for B+-tree design.

solution.result: The key factor is that data transfer from disk to main memory is done in blocks, allowing B+-tree nodes to be packed with many keys per disk read, minimizing I/O operations. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: B+-tree height = O(log_p N) vs BST height = O(log_2 N); large fan-out p is enabled by disk block transfers, making B+-trees optimal for disk-resident database indices.
